pages.raw_text:
Joseph II. Boyd, president Of the
city council and president of the Ma
tional Iron works, was restrained
last night from collecting the amount
of bill for materials furnished the
city. The order was signed by Judge
KEENAN, on complaint of Fred Ehlers
who charged that Mr.. Boyd had been
selling material to the city contrary
to the city charter, which provides
that no councilman or employee of the
city shall be interested in the sale of
supplies used by the city.


The order is to be argued before
Judge KEENAN,, March 10. If Mr.
Boyd is found to be guilty he will
be expelled from the council.


Mr.. Boyd is accused of selling the
city goods in lots over 5000, when
bids were not asked for. And that
during the year 1903, Mr.. Boyd, presi-
dent of the National Iron works,
sold the city 5000 worth of supplies.
1t is further alleged that the price
paid for the supplies was in excess
of their actual worth.


The complaint further says that
by ordinance all bills for goods are
sent to the city auditing committee
to he o. K'd. This committee con-
sists of the president Of the council
Joseph II. Boyd; Mayor L. Frank
Boyd and the city comptroller. Floyd
l.. Daggett. After the auditing COM
mittee has approved a bill it Is sent
to the city council; and, after the
city council has approved a bill, a
warrant is issued for the amount due,
and, under the existing conditions,
the warrant is Immediately paid by
the city.
